title: L3 sync silently truncates at ~1000 rows in non-recency order, dropping brand-new writes from the local cache
source: Claude Fable audit 2026-07-04 (discovered during T-252 candidate triage)
current_state: Discovered while triaging analysis/candidate_tickets.jsonl's flagged testing/test_memory.py failures (test_001_memory_read_after_write, test_001_memory_bulk_read, test_001_single_fact_recall - all fail with 0 results on a fresh MemoryTools instance doing write-then-immediate-read with a real Supabase connection). Root cause reproduced live: _sync_l3() (tools/tools_memory.py, was ~line 2297) ran 'self.supabase.table("l3_active_memory").select("*").execute()' with NO .order() and NO explicit .limit(). Supabase/PostgREST silently caps an unbounded select at ~1000 rows in default (non-recency) order. l3_active_memory currently has 1029 real rows (confirmed via direct count query) - just over the cap. _l3_active_records() triggers _sync_l3() on the first L3 read of any fresh MemoryTools instance with real credentials (self._last_sync is None), and _sync_l3 does DELETE FROM l3_cache then repopulates from the (silently truncated, non-recency-ordered) Supabase response. A live repro confirmed: write a row -> Supabase confirms it exists (_verify_write returns verified:True) -> call _sync_l3() directly -> the row is GONE from local SQLite afterward, and local l3_cache now holds exactly 1000 rows. Filtering by active_until (the archival column) does NOT resolve this today: all 1029 rows are still active (none archived yet), so the growth is genuine, not archival debt. This is the project's #1 recurring bug class (write/read divergence) manifesting in a new place.
target_state: _sync_l3's Supabase query orders by created_at desc and applies an explicit generous limit (_L3_SYNC_ROW_CAP), so if a cap is ever hit again, it deterministically drops the OLDEST rows instead of an arbitrary default-order subset - guaranteeing new writes are always present in the local L3 cache immediately after any sync. FIXED in this session: tools/tools_memory.py __init__ gained self._L3_SYNC_ROW_CAP = 5000 (ponytail comment names the ceiling + upgrade path: incremental delta sync keyed on created_at if this cap is ever hit for real); _sync_l3() now chains .order('created_at', desc=True).limit(self._L3_SYNC_ROW_CAP) before .execute(). Regression test added: testing/test_l3_sync_ordering.py asserts the query builder receives order+limit calls via a recording Supabase double (offline, no live Supabase needed for the assertion). Live re-verification also run: repro script confirms write-then-sync now preserves the row.
migration_plan: 1. Reproduce: write an L3 row with real Supabase creds, confirm Supabase has it (_verify_write), call _sync_l3() directly, confirm the row is missing from local SQLite afterward and local row count == 1000 (silent cap).

2. Fix: in _sync_l3(), change 'self.supabase.table("l3_active_memory").select("*").execute()' to add '.order("created_at", desc=True).limit(self._L3_SYNC_ROW_CAP)' before .execute(); define _L3_SYNC_ROW_CAP=5000 in __init__ near _sync_ttl_seconds with a ponytail comment naming the ceiling and upgrade path.

3. Test: testing/test_l3_sync_ordering.py - a recording Supabase double captures the .order()/.limit() calls made by _sync_l3(); assert order key is created_at desc and limit is the configured cap. Offline, no live Supabase needed.
4. Live re-verification (real Supabase, cheap - no LLM cost): rerun the exact repro from step 1 against the fixed code; confirm the just-written row survives the sync this time.
5. python scripts/verify.py PASS; append solutions/SOLUTIONS.jsonl; move this ticket to closed/.
risk_notes: This bug is invisible to scripts/verify.py's non-costly gate because testing/test_memory.py is deliberately excluded (COSTLY_TESTS - hits real Supabase) and test_memory_roundtrip_contract.py's keystone-gate L3 test runs OFFLINE against a noop Supabase (never triggers _sync_l3's real-Supabase branch at all), so this class of regression has no free-tier safety net. Consider (separately, not in this ticket's scope) whether the keystone gate should grow a cheap real-Supabase L3 sync check now that this exact failure mode is understood - flagged for T-266/future memory-hygiene work, not fixed here to keep this diff bounded to the reproduced bug.
